AI agents just got a new brain and it's fast. Like really fast. GLM5 Turbo just dropped and it is changing the way developers, builders, and automation nerds are running their AI systems. We're not talking about a chatbot upgrade. We're talking about AI that reads your whole codebase, plans what to build, writes the code, runs the tests, fixes the bugs, and deploys the thing all on its own. The best part, it costs a fraction of what you're probably paying right now. Now, if you've ever hit a wall with AI agents being too slow, too expensive, or cutting out mid task, this is the video for you. GLM5 Turbo just tripled usage limits, runs at insane speed, and works inside the exact agent workflows you're already trying to build. Stick around because by the end of this video, you're going to understand exactly how this model works, why it matters, and how to use it to automate real tasks in your business. Today, let's go. Hey, if we haven't met already, I'm the digital avatar of Julian Goldie, CEO of SEO agency Goldie Agency. Whilst he's helping clients get more leads and customers, I'm here to help you get the latest AI updates. Julian Goldie reads every comment, so make sure you comment below and let him know what you think about GLM5 Turbo. Now, let's get into it. Here's something most people don't talk about. AI agents are powerful. Everyone knows that. You can point one at a task and it starts working. planning steps, using tools, writing code, browsing the web. But here's the problem. They hit limits fast. You're running an agent that's building something for you. It's mid task. Things are going great. Then it just stops. You've used up your prompts. You've hit the wall. And now you either wait or you pay more. That's been the bottleneck for everyone serious about AI automation. The models that were fast enough to run agents were expensive. The models that were affordable were too slow. And the usage limits made it impossible to run anything at serious scale. And it's not just developers hitting this wall. Business owners trying to automate their workflows. Content creators trying to build systems. Marketers trying to run campaigns on autopilot. Everyone who's tried to scale AI beyond a single chat window has felt this friction. You start with a great idea, you set up the agent, you watch it work, and then it runs out of runway before it finishes the job. GLM5 Turbo solves all three of those problems at once. Let me break this down simple. GLM5 is a brand new AI model built from the ground up for agentic engineering. Not just answering questions, actually doing work, long tasks, multi-step tasks, the kind of work that used to require a whole team of developers. GLM5 Turbo is the high-speed version of that model. It's optimized for agent workflows, coding agents, automation pipelines, and multi-tool tasks. It trades a tiny bit of reasoning depth for massive speed and scale, which is exactly what you want when an AI agent is running thousands of actions. The architecture behind it is a mixture of experts model around 744 billion total parameters with about 40 billion active at any one time. Context window of around 200,000 tokens. That means it can read an entire codebase, a full documentation library, or months of chat history all in one go without forgetting anything. That's not just impressive. That's genuinely useful for real automation work. Here's the announcement that got everyone's attention. The usage limits just tripled. On the light plan, you're looking at around 80 prompts per 5 hours. Pro gets you around 400 prompts per 5 hours. And the max plan goes up to around 1,600 prompts per 5 hours. And here's the thing, each one of those prompts can include multiple model calls. So, in practice, you're dealing with billions of tokens per month at the top tier. That's enough to run full AI agent workflows all day long without hitting a wall. Real scale, real throughput without the constant interruptions that kill momentum. This is where it gets interesting. Speed and scale matter, but so does cost. If you're running high volume AI agents, the pricing adds up fast. Some of the most capable models out there cost around $5 per million input tokens. GLM5 comes in at around $1 per million tokens. That's five times cheaper, and output tokens are even cheaper. On top of that, for anyone building AI systems that run constantly, automating tasks, generating content, processing data, managing workflows, that difference is the gap between something being expensive to run and something being practically free to run at scale. That's why developers building serious agent systems are paying very close attention to GLM5 Turbo right now. Let me make this concrete with a real example. Say you want to build a full landing page for the AI profit boardroom. Not a rough draft, a proper high converting page that explains what AI automation is, why business owners need it right now, and why the community is the best place to learn it. You'd give your GLM5 Turbo agent a prompt like this. Build a landing page for the AI profit boardroom. The goal is to convert cold traffic into leads. Include a headline that speaks directly to business owners who want to save time using AI. Add sections on what the community teaches, who it's for, and the key benefits of learning AI automation today. Keep the copy punchy, simple, and benefitled. The agent reads that, then plans the structure, writes the copy, builds the HTML, styles it, tests it, fixes any issues, hands you back a working, ready to deploy landing page. All of it done autonomously. You could run the same kind of workflow to write and schedule your email sequences, build lead magnets, create onboarding flows for new members, automate your content pipeline, generate your social post for the week. The model handles the execution. You handle the direction. That's what AI automation actually looks like when it's working properly. One more feature worth knowing about. GLM5 has two modes, thinking mode and non-thinking mode. Thinking mode is slower and goes deep. is good for complex problems where you need the model to really work through the logic before acting. Architecture decisions, debugging tricky code, planning a system with a lot of moving parts, non-thinking mode is fast and direct, perfect for repetitive agent tasks where speed matters more than extra deliberation, writing content, generating variations, processing data, running tasks that don't need the model to stop and reason. They just need to get done. You can toggle between them depending on what your workflow needs. That flexibility is huge if you're running mixed workloads. Some tasks that need precision, others that just need to move fast. Most agent systems benefit from a mix of both. And now you have full control over which mode fires when. Here's what I want you to walk away understanding. We are in the middle of a genuine shift in how software gets built and how businesses get run. Not just AI that helps you write things faster. Not just a smarter autocomplete. Not just a better chatbot. We're moving toward AI agents that do real engineering work that handle entire workflows autonomously that build, test, deploy, and iterate without a human in the loop for every step. That run your content, that manage your outreach, that keep your systems running while you focus on the bigger picture. That's the world GLM5 Turbo was built for. Fast enough to run at scale, cheap enough to use without blowing your budget, powerful enough to handle complex multi-step tasks across real business workflows.
